title: Babesiosis
section: Protozoal and Helminthic Infections General Considerations

items.title: Treatment Strategy for Babesiosis
items.content: Babesiosis is typically treated with a standard course of atovaquone plus azithromycin. However, highly immunocompromised patients and asplenic individuals require prolonged treatment due to their increased risk of persistent infection.
keyPoints: Standard treatment: atovaquone plus azithromycin
Highly immunocompromised and asplenic individuals need longer treatment duration
Adjunct red cell exchange may be beneficial in severe cases
items.reference: Babesiosis · Edouard Vannier, Jeffrey A. Gelfand

items.title: Prevention of Babesiosis
items.content: In the absence of a vaccine and chemoprophylaxis for babesiosis, individuals at risk should minimize tick exposure and avoid endemic areas to prevent infection.
keyPoints: No available vaccine or chemoprophylaxis
Minimize exposure to ticks
Avoid endemic areas if possible
items.reference: Babesiosis · Edouard Vannier, Jeffrey A. Gelfand

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.title: Geographic Distribution and Predominant Etiologic Agents of Babesiosis in the United States
items.content: Babesiosis cases in the U.S. are predominantly caused by B. microti, especially in the Northeast and upper Midwest regions. Other species like B. duncani and B. divergens-like organisms have been reported in specific states.
keyPoints: Most (>95%) U.S. babesiosis cases are due to B. microti.
Predominant areas: Northeast (e.g., Massachusetts, Rhode Island) and upper Midwest (e.g., Minnesota, Wisconsin).
B. duncani reported in Washington State, Oregon, California.
B. divergens-like organisms documented in Arkansas, Missouri, Michigan, Kentucky, Washington State, Pennsylvania.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.title: Incidence and Reporting of Babesiosis Cases
items.content: The incidence of babesiosis has increased over time due to factors such as tick density and geographic expansion. The CDC receives weekly reports from state health departments.
keyPoints: Babesiosis is a nationally notifiable disease since 2011.
In 2023, more than 3200 cases reported from 33 states.
In 2011, 1126 cases were reported to the CDC.
Increase in incidence attributed to greater tick density and northward expansion.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.title: Modes of Transmission for Babesiosis
items.content: Babesiosis is primarily transmitted through tick bites and blood transfusions. The American Red Cross began screening in high-risk areas to prevent transfusion-transmitted babesiosis.
keyPoints: Tick bite transmission: B. microti via I. scapularis ticks, nymphs active from late spring to early summer.
Blood transfusion transmission: Over 300 cases of TTB reported; most involve packed RBCs.
B. microti viable throughout RBC unit's shelf life (4-42 days).
ARC began screening blood donations in May 2020 in high-risk areas, reducing suspected TTB cases.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.title: Vertical Transmission and Neonatal Babesiosis
items.content: Babesia microti can be transmitted vertically across the placenta, though it is rare. Symptoms in neonates typically develop between the third to sixth week of life, presenting as fever, pallor, and lethargy with parasitemia ranging from 2% to 5%. Most cases are acquired through blood transfusion or tick bites.
keyPoints: Vertical transmission across placenta is rare but documented.
Symptoms in neonates: fever, pallor, lethargy; appear third to sixth week of life.
Parasitemia at presentation ranges from 2% to 5%.
Neonatal babesiosis primarily acquired through blood transfusion or tick bite.
<1% of annual babesiosis cases in infants (<1 year) reported to CDC.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.title: Babesiosis Transmission via Solid Organ Transplantation
items.content: A single case report highlights babesiosis transmission through solid organ transplantation. Two patients developed babesiosis 8 weeks post-kidney allograft from a donor with multiple transfusions, suggesting red blood cells in the donated kidneys were the infection source.
keyPoints: Babesiosis can be transmitted via kidney transplantation.
Infection diagnosed 8 weeks after transplantation.
RBCs in vasculature/fluids of donated kidneys likely source.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.title: Geographical Distribution and Risk Factors for Babesiosis
items.content: Babesiosis is more common in Europe and Asia, with specific species linked to different regions. In Europe, B. divergens is prevalent in France and Ireland, while B. microti has emerged as an infectious disease in China. Major risk factors include advanced age (≥50 years), tick exposure, asplenia, and immunosuppression.
keyPoints: B. divergens cases reported mainly in France and Ireland since 1957.
In Europe, most patients lack a spleen or have functional hyposplenism.
B. microti recognized as human pathogen in Taiwan; emerging in China.
Major risk factors: advanced age (≥50 years), tick exposure, asplenia, immunosuppression.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Transmission and Global Incidence of Babesiosis
items.content: Babesiosis caused by B. microti has been reported in various regions outside the United States, including Canada, Mexico, Colombia, India, and Australia. The disease is reportable in some areas like Manitoba and Quebec.
keyPoints: Three cases of babesiosis due to B. microti reported from Canada (southern Manitoba, southern Nova Scotia).
Asymptomatic infections detected in blood donors from southwestern Ontario.
Babesiosis is a reportable disease in Manitoba and Quebec.
Four febrile illness cases attributed to B. microti in Yucatan, Mexico.
Evidence of Babesia bovis and Babesia bigemina causing human illness in Uraba, Colombia.
Death from novel Babesia species in a splenectomized farmer in western India.
Fatal case of B. microti infection reported from New South Wales, Australia.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Diagnosis of B. microti Infection
items.content: Symptoms of B. microti infection typically appear 1-4 weeks post-tick bite or 3-7 weeks after transfusion, with fever reaching up to 40.9°C. Severe cases may present with hemolytic anemia and complications like splenic infarction.
keyPoints: Symptoms: fatigue, malaise, fever (up to 40.9°C), chills, night sweats, headache, myalgia, anorexia.
Less common symptoms: arthralgia, nausea, dry cough, neck stiffness, emotional lability, sore throat.
Dark urine and jaundice indicate severe hemolytic anemia.
Physical exam may show fever, pale/yellowish skin, focal red rash (tick bite site), ecchymoses, petechiae, scleral icterus.
Splenomegaly suggested by left upper quadrant tenderness; splenic infarction or rupture suspected with hypotension and tachycardia.
CT confirms splenic infarcts (wedge-shaped hypodense lesions) and hemoperitoneum (hyperdense fluid around spleen).
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Severe Babesiosis: Complications and Outcomes
items.content: Severe babesiosis often requires hospitalization with complications like acute renal failure (20%) and respiratory failure (7%). Risk factors include diagnosis >7 days after symptom onset, asplenia, and autoimmune disorders.
keyPoints: Median hospital stay: 4 days (interquartile range, 3–7).
Complications: acute renal failure (20%), acute respiratory failure (7%), arrhythmia, heart failure, DIC, shock.
1 in 5 hospitalized patients receives RBC transfusion.
Diagnosis >7 days after symptoms increases risk of severe disease requiring ICU admission.
Diarrhea and nausea/vomiting predict severe babesiosis.
Asplenia and autoimmune disorders predispose to severity; cardiac conditions do not.
Fatality rates: 0.6% (2011-2015 cases), higher in ≥65 years, immunocompromised (~20%), splenic rupture/infarction (6%).
Prior treatment with clindamycin plus quinine had fatality rates of 5%-9%; atovaquone plus azithromycin is now first-line therapy.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Splenectomy as a Risk Factor for Severe Babesiosis
items.content: Individuals without a spleen or with functional hyposplenism are at higher risk of severe babesiosis, particularly from B. divergens in Europe and B. crassa-like infections.
keyPoints: B. divergens infection often leads to renal failure and respiratory distress without immediate therapy.
All 5 patients infected with B. venatorum in Europe were splenectomized; their illness ranged from mild to severe, none died.
Severe cases of B. crassa-like infection occurred in splenectomized individuals in Europe.
items.reference: Babesiosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Pathogenesis and Complications of Babesiosis
items.content: Babesiosis pathogenesis involves hemolysis leading to renal failure, vascular inflammation, splenic infarction, and potential for hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis.
keyPoints: Hemolysis causes RBC debris accumulation in kidneys, potentially causing renal failure.
Excessive hemolysis consumes nitric oxide, leading to thrombus formation and vascular inflammation.
RBC lysis can cause splenic infarction due to localized necrosis.
Massive phagocytosis of blood cells may lead to hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis.
items.reference: Babesiosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Immunological Aspects and Treatment Considerations in Babesiosis
items.content: Protective immunity against babesiosis involves CD4+ T cells, with severe disease observed in immunocompromised individuals. Antibodies may play a role in parasite clearance.
keyPoints: CD4+ T cells, particularly Th1 cells, are crucial for protective immunity against babesiosis.
Severe babesiosis is more common in patients with AIDS or those who have undergone allografts.
Persistent babesiosis can occur post-rituximab treatment, suggesting a role for B cells and antibodies.
items.reference: Babesiosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: When to Consider Babesiosis in Patients
items.content: Babesiosis should be considered for patients with compatible symptoms and potential tick exposure, particularly from late spring to early fall or recent blood transfusion history. It is also relevant in Lyme disease patients whose symptoms persist despite treatment.
keyPoints: Consider babesiosis if there's possible tick exposure or recent packed RBC transfusion.
Evaluate for babesiosis in persistent Lyme disease cases.
One-half of babesiosis patients are co-infected with B. burgdorferi.
items.reference: Babesiosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Laboratory Findings and Predictors of Severe Babesiosis
items.content: Routine lab tests for babesiosis include CBC showing anemia, elevated reticulocytes, low haptoglobin, elevated LDH, thrombocytopenia, altered WBC counts, liver enzyme elevations, and renal markers. Specific thresholds predict severe disease.
keyPoints: Anemia with elevated reticulocyte count indicates stress erythropoiesis.
Severe babesiosis predictors: total bilirubin >1.9 mg/dL, WBC <5 × 10³/µL (better prognosis), WBC >10 × 10³/µL, creatinine >1.2 mg/dL.
Alkaline phosphatase >125 IU/L and WBC >5 × 10³/µL predict severe disease.
items.reference: Babesiosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Specific Testing for Babesiosis
items.content: A definitive diagnosis of babesiosis is made by microscopic examination or PCR amplification of Babesia DNA. Microscopy identifies characteristic trophozoites and tetrads, while PCR detects low parasitemia levels.
keyPoints: Definitive diagnosis via Giemsa-stained blood smears or PCR.
Tetrad formation is pathognomonic for babesiosis.
Parasitemia >4% indicates risk of severe disease; splenic complications can occur with low-grade parasitemia.
items.reference: Babesiosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Treatment Protocol for Mild to Moderate Babesiosis
items.content: For mild to moderate babesiosis caused by *Babesia microti*, the recommended treatment is atovaquone plus azithromycin administered orally over 7–10 days. Symptoms typically improve within 48 hours of starting therapy and resolve within 1–2 weeks.
keyPoints: Treatment: Atovaquone plus azithromycin for 7–10 days
Symptom improvement: Usually within 48 hours
Resolution time: Within 1–2 weeks
items.reference: Babesiosis · MILD TO MODERATE B. MICROTILLNESS

items.title: Management of Persistent Symptoms in Babesiosis
items.content: If symptoms persist despite initial therapy for babesiosis, it is crucial to initiate doxycycline and test for other tick-borne pathogens such as *Borrelia burgdorferi*, *Anaplasma phagocytophilum*, or *Babesia miyamotoi*. Fatigue may continue for weeks to months but does not justify extending or resuming treatment. Follow-up PCR testing is not recommended due to the unlikelihood of relapse in immunocompetent individuals.
keyPoints: Persistent symptoms: Initiate doxycycline and test for other tick-borne pathogens
Fatigue duration: Weeks to months, does not warrant extended treatment
Follow-up PCR testing: Not recommended due to unlikely relapse
items.reference: Babesiosis · MILD TO MODERATE B. MICROTILLNESS

items.title: First-Line Antimicrobial Therapy for Severe Babesiosis
items.content: Oral atovaquone plus IV azithromycin is the preferred regimen for treating severe babesiosis caused by B. microti, supported by retrospective studies showing high efficacy and fewer adverse events compared to clindamycin plus quinine.
keyPoints: Preferred regimen: oral atovaquone plus IV azithromycin
Retrospective study of 40 patients; 39 improved with this treatment
Adverse events: 15% for atovaquone plus azithromycin, 72% for clindamycin plus quinine
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 1)

items.title: Criteria and Use of RBC Exchange in Severe Babesiosis
items.content: RBC exchange is recommended for high parasitemia (>10%) with clinical deterioration, especially in cases of severe hemolysis or organ dysfunction. It aims to reduce parasite burden rapidly but should not be solely based on parasitemia levels.
keyPoints: Initiate RCE when parasitemia >10%
RCE reduces parasite burden and corrects anemia
Post-RCE parasitemia does not correlate with mortality or hospital stay length
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 1)

items.title: Management of Babesiosis in Highly Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: In immunocompromised patients, antimicrobial therapy should last at least 6 weeks, with azithromycin maintained at 500 mg/d to prevent resistance. Monitoring includes daily labs until parasitemia is <4%, followed by PCR testing.
keyPoints: Therapy duration: at least 6 weeks
Azithromycin dosage: maintain at 500 mg/d in immunocompromised patients
Monitor with blood smear and real-time PCR
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS
items.title: Management and Resistance in Severe Babesiosis
items.content: In severe babesiosis, particularly with B. microti, managing antimicrobial resistance is crucial. When resistance to atovaquone or azithromycin is suspected due to mutations, alternative treatments like clindamycin plus quinine can be used. Tafenoquine may substitute for drugs targeting specific gene products, but it should not be prescribed to patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
keyPoints: Antimicrobial resistance in B. microti can occur due to mutations in cytochrome b (Cytb) or ribosomal genes.
Clindamycin plus quinine is an alternative when atovaquone and azithromycin fail, especially if quinine toxicity is a concern.
Tafenoquine acts by inducing reactive oxygen species and can substitute for drugs targeting specific gene products.
Tafenoquine loading dose: 600 mg over 3 days; maintenance: 300 mg weekly for 5 weeks post last positive PCR test.
Do not prescribe tafenoquine to patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS
items.title: Approach to Splenic Rupture in Babesiosis
items.content: Splenic rupture, a complication of babesiosis, typically occurs in young, healthy patients with low-grade parasitemia. Management depends on hemodynamic stability: emergent splenectomy for instability, arterial embolization if stable but bleeding persists, and non-surgical management without hemoperitoneum.
keyPoints: Splenic rupture is common in young, healthy patients with low-grade parasitemia.
Emergent splenectomy is required for hemodynamically unstable patients.
Consider splenic arterial embolization if stable but bleeding persists.
Non-surgical management is appropriate without hemoperitoneum, with careful monitoring.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS

items.question: Which organism is the primary cause of babesiosis in the United States?
items.explanation: Babesia microti is the species typically found in small rodents and transmitted by Ixodes scapularis, causing most cases of babesiosis in the United States.
items.reference: Babesiosis · Edouard Vannier, Jeffrey A. Gelfand

items.question: What is the standard treatment for most patients with babesiosis?
items.explanation: A single standard course of atovaquone plus azithromycin is sufficient to achieve cure for most patients with babesiosis.
items.reference: Babesiosis · Edouard Vannier, Jeffrey A. Gelfand

items.question: Which group of individuals should be treated for a longer duration due to the risk of persistent infection?
items.explanation: Highly immunocompromised patients and asplenic individuals are at risk of persistent infection and should be treated for a longer duration.
items.reference: Babesiosis · Edouard Vannier, Jeffrey A. Gelfand

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.question: Which Babesia species is the predominant etiologic agent of human babesiosis in the United States?
items.explanation: Most cases of babesiosis in the United States are caused by B. microti.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary vector responsible for transmitting B. microti to humans?
items.explanation: B. microti is primarily transmitted by the Ixodes scapularis tick.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.question: Which factor does NOT contribute to the rise in the incidence of babesiosis?
items.explanation: The poor ecologic fitness of B. microti compared to B. burgdorferi contributes to the rise, not the ecologic fitness of B. burgdorferi.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.question: Which blood component has NOT been implicated in transfusion-transmitted babesiosis?
items.explanation: No cases of transfusion-transmitted babesiosis have been attributed to apheresis platelets.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is a documented but rare mode of transmission for B. microti?
items.explanation: Vertical transmission of B. microti across the placenta has been documented but is rare.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.question: In which region outside the United States are most cases of babesiosis caused by B. divergens reported?
items.explanation: Most cases of babesiosis caused by B. divergens occur in France and Ireland.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.question: Which vector is responsible for transmitting B. microti and B. venatorum in eastern Poland?
items.explanation: I. ricinus is the vector for B. microti and B. venatorum.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is a major risk factor for severe babesiosis?
items.explanation: Major risk factors for severe babesiosis include advanced age, tick exposure, and asplenia.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following regions has reported cases of babesiosis caused by B. microti?
items.explanation: Cases of babesiosis caused by B. microti have been reported from Canada.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.question: What is the typical time frame for symptoms to appear after a tick bite in B. microti infection?
items.explanation: Symptoms typically appear 1–4 weeks after the bite of an infected tick.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.question: Which complication is most common in severe babesiosis?
items.explanation: The leading complication of severe babesiosis is acute renal failure (20%).
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.question: What is a strong predictor of severe babesiosis?
items.explanation: Diarrhea and nausea or vomiting are strong predictors of severe babesiosis.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a common symptom of B. divergens infection?
items.explanation: B. divergens infection commonly presents with fever greater than 41°C.
items.reference: Babesiosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What is a major risk factor for severe babesiosis?
items.explanation: The spleen plays a critical role in clearing parasitized RBCs, making asplenia a major risk factor for severe disease.
items.reference: Babesiosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: In the context of babesiosis pathogenesis, what is one consequence of excessive hemolysis?
items.explanation: Excessive hemolysis leads to free hemoglobin scavenging and consuming nitric oxide, resulting in vascular inflammation.
items.reference: Babesiosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which cell type is crucial for protective immunity against babesiosis?
items.explanation: Protective immunity involves CD4+ T cells, particularly Th1 cells.
items.reference: Babesiosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which laboratory finding is consistent with hemolysis in babesiosis?
items.explanation: Low levels of haptoglobin are consistent with hemolysis.
items.reference: Babesiosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is a pathognomonic feature of babesiosis on blood smear?
options: Ring form lacking central hemozoin deposit
items.explanation: The ring form in babesiosis lacks the central brownish (hemozoin) deposit typical of Plasmodium falciparum.
items.reference: Babesiosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition should be considered for diagnosis when a patient with babesiosis has worsening symptoms despite appropriate antibiotic therapy?
items.explanation: Babesiosis should be considered in Lyme disease patients whose symptoms worsen or do not abate with antibiotic therapy.
items.reference: Babesiosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the recommended test for definitive diagnosis of babesiosis when parasites are not visible by microscopy?
items.explanation: Amplification of Babesia DNA using real-time PCR is recommended when parasites are not visible by microscopy.
items.reference: Babesiosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the standard treatment regimen for mild to moderate babesiosis caused by Babesia microti?
options: Atovaquone plus azithromycin administered orally for 7–10 days
items.explanation: Atovaquone plus azithromycin is the recommended treatment for mild to moderate babesiosis caused by B. microti.
items.reference: Babesiosis · MILD TO MODERATE B. MICROTILLNESS

items.question: If symptoms of babesiosis persist despite initial therapy, what additional steps should be taken?
options: Extend the duration of atovaquone and azithromycin treatment
Initiate doxycycline while testing for other tick-borne pathogens
items.explanation: If symptoms persist, initiating doxycycline and testing for other tick-borne pathogens is recommended.
items.reference: Babesiosis · MILD TO MODERATE B. MICROTILLNESS

items.question: What should be done if parasite DNA is detected up to 3 months after treatment for babesiosis?
options: Resume or extend the initial treatment regimen
items.explanation: Follow-up PCR testing is not recommended because relapse in immunocompetent individuals is unlikely.
items.reference: Babesiosis · MILD TO MODERATE B. MICROTILLNESS

items.question: What is the preferred first-line antimicrobial therapy for severe babesiosis caused by B. microti?
items.explanation: The preferred regimen for severe babesiosis caused by B. microti is oral atovaquone plus IV azithromycin.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 1)

items.question: Which adverse events are associated with the use of quinine in the treatment of babesiosis?
items.explanation: Use of quinine is impeded by a risk for QTc prolongation and cinchonism, including tinnitus.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended duration of antimicrobial therapy for highly immunocompromised patients with babesiosis?
options: 2 weeks beyond the first definitive negative PCR test
items.explanation: Antimicrobial therapy should be administered for at least 6 consecutive weeks, including 2 weeks beyond the first definitive negative PCR test.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 1)

items.question: What is a major risk factor for persistent, relapsing babesiosis in immunocompromised patients?
items.explanation: Major risk factors for persistent, relapsing babesiosis include asplenia.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS
items.question: Which combination is used to manage patients who relapse while or after being treated with atovaquone plus azithromycin?
items.explanation: Some patients who relapse while or after being treated with atovaquone plus azithromycin have been managed with clindamycin plus quinine.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS
items.question: What is the rationale for substituting atovaquone-proguanil for atovaquone when resistance to atovaquone is predicted to be partial?
options: Proguanil increases the concentration of atovaquone required.
Proguanil lowers the concentration of atovaquone required.
Proguanil targets a different gene product.
items.explanation: As with P. falciparum, proguanil lowers the concentration of atovaquone required to collapse the mitochondrial membrane potential.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS
items.question: Why should tafenoquine not be prescribed to patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency?
options: Their RBCs have increased antioxidant capacity.
Their RBCs have reduced antioxidant capacity.
Tafenoquine is ineffective against B. microti.
Tafenoquine increases the risk of splenic rupture.
items.explanation: Patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency have RBCs with reduced antioxidant capacity and would not sustain the oxidative stress caused by tafenoquine.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS

items.statement: Babesiosis is primarily transmitted by the deer tick, Ixodes scapularis.
items.explanation: The passage states that Babesia microti is transmitted by the deer tick, Ixodes scapularis.
items.reference: Babesiosis · Edouard Vannier, Jeffrey A. Gelfand

items.statement: A vaccine for babesiosis is currently available.
items.explanation: The passage mentions the absence of a vaccine and chemoprophylaxis for babesiosis.
items.reference: Babesiosis · Edouard Vannier, Jeffrey A. Gelfand

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.statement: B. microti can be transmitted transovarially in ticks.
items.explanation: Tick larvae are not infected because B. microti is not transmitted transovarially.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.statement: The incidence of transfusion-transmitted babesiosis has decreased since the implementation of blood donation screening.
items.explanation: No suspected TTB cases were reported after the American Red Cross began testing all blood donations in high-risk areas.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.statement: Most cases of neonatal babesiosis are acquired through vertical transmission.
items.explanation: Most cases of neonatal babesiosis are acquired through blood transfusion or tick bite.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.statement: Ixodes ricinus is the vector for B. microti in Europe.
items.explanation: I. ricinus is indeed the vector for B. microti in Europe.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.statement: Concurrent Lyme disease increases the risk of severe babesiosis.
items.explanation: Concurrent Lyme disease does not decrease the risk of severe disease but is associated with a lower risk for hospitalization.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.statement: Atovaquone plus azithromycin is the first-line therapy for babesiosis.
items.explanation: Since atovaquone plus azithromycin has become the first-line therapy, a fatal outcome is rare.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Persistent anemia in babesiosis is often due to autoantibodies tagging RBCs for clearance.
items.explanation: Autoantibodies can tag RBCs for clearance, contributing to persistent anemia.
items.reference: Babesiosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: B. venatorum infection is always severe in spleen-intact individuals.
items.explanation: In Europe, B. venatorum infections were mild to severe in splenectomized patients but not necessarily severe in spleen-intact individuals.
items.reference: Babesiosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: A single positive serologic result is sufficient to establish a diagnosis of babesiosis.
items.explanation: A single positive serologic result is not sufficient due to persistent antibodies after infection resolution.
items.reference: Babesiosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Parasitemia levels above 4% are a risk factor for severe, complicated babesiosis.
items.explanation: Parasitemia of >4% is indeed a risk factor for severe disease.
items.reference: Babesiosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Fatigue after treatment for babesiosis should prompt an extension of therapy.
items.explanation: Fatigue may persist but does not warrant extending or resuming treatment.
items.reference: Babesiosis · MILD TO MODERATE B. MICROTILLNESS

items.statement: Symptoms of babesiosis typically resolve within 1–2 weeks after starting therapy.
items.explanation: Symptoms usually abate within 48 hours and resolve within 1–2 weeks.
items.reference: Babesiosis · MILD TO MODERATE B. MICROTILLNESS

items.statement: RCE is recommended when parasitemia is >10%.
items.explanation: It is common practice to initiate RCE when parasitemia is >10%.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 1)

items.statement: Repeat RCE is advocated based on post-RCE parasitemia levels.
items.explanation: Post-RCE parasitemia was associated neither with post-RCE length of hospital stay nor with mortality, advocating against the use of repeat RCE.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS
items.statement: Splenic rupture in babesiosis is typically managed with emergent splenectomy if the patient is hemodynamically unstable.
items.explanation: If the patient is hemodynamically unstable, emergent splenectomy should be performed.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS
items.statement: Tafenoquine maintenance therapy for babesiosis is continued indefinitely after the last positive PCR test.
items.explanation: Maintenance therapy with tafenoquine was discontinued 5 weeks after the last positive B. microti PCR test.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 2) | OTHER BABESIA INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.assertion: The geographic expansion of babesiosis has lagged behind that of Lyme disease.
items.reason: B. microti has poor ecologic fitness compared to B. burgdorferi.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.assertion: Most cases of babesiosis present from June through August.
items.reason: This is because adult ticks are the primary vector during this period.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; nymphs are the primary vectors responsible for transmission in this period.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.assertion: Babesiosis cases caused by B. divergens-like parasites have been reported in the Canary Islands.
items.reason: Ixodes ricinus is the vector for B. divergens-like parasites.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but Ixodes ricinus being a vector does not explain the occurrence of cases on the Canary Islands.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.assertion: Babesiosis is an emerging infectious disease in mainland China.
items.reason: B. microti was recognized as a human pathogen in Taiwan in the late 1990s.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the recognition of B. microti in Taiwan does not explain its emergence in mainland China.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.reason: Underlying cardiac conditions do not predispose to severe babesiosis.
items.explanation: Both asplenia and the lack of predisposition by cardiac conditions are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)
items.assertion: Fatality rates for babesiosis were higher before atovaquone plus azithromycin became the first-line therapy.
items.reason: Clindamycin plus quinine was used prior to atovaquone plus azithromycin, with fatality rates ranging from 5% to 9%.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Babesiosis · ETIOLOGY, EPIDEMIOLOGY, AND MODES OF TRANSMISSION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Asplenia is a major risk factor for severe babesiosis.
items.reason: The spleen is the immunodominant organ in clearing parasitized RBCs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why asplenia increases the risk of severe disease.
items.reference: Babesiosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: CD4+ T cells play a minor role in protective immunity against babesiosis.
items.reason: Protective immunity involves CD4+ T cells, particularly Th1 cells.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because CD4+ T cells are crucial for protective immunity.
items.reference: Babesiosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: A definitive diagnosis of babesiosis requires microscopic examination or PCR.
items.reason: Microscopic examination and amplification of Babesia DNA are necessary for confirming the presence of parasites.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Babesiosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: An elevated reticulocyte count indicates severe babesiosis.
items.reason: An elevated reticulocyte count signifies stress-induced erythropoiesis due to hemolysis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not specifically indicate severity of babesiosis.
items.reference: Babesiosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Atovaquone plus azithromycin is preferred over clindamycin plus quinine for severe babesiosis.
items.reason: Adverse events were reported by a higher percentage of patients treated with clindamycin plus quinine compared to atovaquone plus azithromycin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the preference.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 1)

items.assertion: RCE should be withheld when parasitemia has begun to resolve following initiation of antimicrobial therapy.
items.reason: Pre-RCE parasitemia is a predictor of post-RCE length of hospital stay but not mortality.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason provided does not explain why RCE should be withheld.
items.reference: Babesiosis · SEVERE B. MICROTI LLLNESS (part 1)
